tweets.text:
Midterm seasonality exists in the stock market because it's common for sitting US presidents to cater to populist desires to influence the average voter. This year's path has been different and there can be infinite reasons why that is the case and nothing revolves around historical seasonality anyways.

Analyzing Trump's actions this year also raises the question around his incorporation of authoritarian elements to his midterm strategy as well. Think Netanyahu and Erdogan. Fostering global conflicts to push the security narrative and stoke fear, advancing legislation to solidify core constituency and appealing to nationalism/protectionism to divide. These things are also generally not that great for markets.

Political judgements aside, we are entering the teeth of that negative midterm seasonality.

tweets.text: Progress is invisible for most in today's world, and it happens every single day let me give you an example. The day a disease stops taking children, or we find a way to give communities access to clean water you won't see parades globally. We built a world of quiet miracles and then complained that it felt ordinary. Ordinary is the miracle....

tweets.text:
Friedberg: New study shows enzyme reverses skin age from 70+ years to 31

Chamath: That’s a $2T market.

Did Scientists Just Discover How to Reverse Skin Aging!?

“CML is kind of the predominant molecule that gets formed in this extracellular matrix that's driving aging, and nothing breaks it down.

So these scientists set out to try and create an enzyme, an enzyme is a protein that breaks something down, that can break down CML.

And so these guys kind of went out and they took the target, which is CML, and tried to figure out, ‘Okay, how do we actually degrade CML, clear that extracellular matrix, and reverse aging?’

They started to test it on the proteins that we would find in our body, casein, collagen, retinal proteins, which are in your eye, hemoglobin, and they were able to get rid of 52 to 97% of the CML, just degrade it away.

And then they found several sites where they were able to degrade over 90%.

And then they took actual human skin from elderly patients that had donated their skin, and they put this enzyme onto that skin, and they were able to eliminate 55% of the CML on the skin, basically reverse the skin's age down to the age of a 31-year-old, this is from greater than 70-year-old patients, just by putting this enzyme on the skin.

And so it's kind of a groundbreaking demonstration of combination of AlphaFold, what's called directed evolution, where you change the order of the DNA that changes the structure of the protein to test different proteins, do high throughput screening, and ultimately make a novel protein that doesn't exist in nature today that can do something pretty profound for human health.”

Chamath:

“It will be a trillion-dollar market. If you can create a cream…”

Friedberg:

“I mean, dude, if you could put this enzyme literally on your skin and have it absorb in…”

Chamath:

“Game over. That alone is $2 trillion.”

tweets.text:
Galaxy Stadium is the new home of Red Raider Football. 🏈

@TechAthletics is a storied program, from a Big 12 title to one of the premier home-field environments in college football.  As the official data center and digital assets partner of the Red Raiders, we're proud to be part of that story.

We've been building and investing in nearby Dickens County, where @galaxyhq's Helios campus now carries 1.6 GW of capacity powering the infrastructure behind the code economy.  Texas Tech grads are already on our team there, and for the next generation of talent, this is a direct pipeline into the AI and digital assets industry.

#WreckEmTech

tweets.text:
The biggest disinflationary shock in history is close.

AI and robotics aren't just filling the productivity gap left by an aging workforce. They're pulling down the cost of everything at the same time.

You can already see it. A few years ago you'd rarely see an autonomous vehicle outside a handful of test cities... now they run commercial fleets across the US, coming for rideshare and taxis. Every one of them is a driver who doesn't sleep, unionise, or ask for a raise.

When China joined the WTO it flooded the world with cheap labour and held inflation down for two decades. What's coming now is far bigger.

People are accepting it faster than any technology in history, and the reason is simple. There is no other choice.

tweets.text:
Why would you ever sell anything?

We are living through the fastest acceleration of technology in all of human history. The financial system is being rebuilt on crypto rails: agentic identity, robotic identity, human identity, all of it moving onchain.

And yet people are still trying to time it. Selling because someone somewhere thinks the liquidity cycle might soften for a bit is bananas.

You don't time a technology adoption cycle. You accumulate through it. Every drawdown is a chance to own more of the base layer of the next economy.

The one job is to stay in.

@BittelJulien and I got into all of this on The Journey Man. As ever, please enjoy!

I think it’ll be a fight between:

- Objectiveness and publicly verifiable evidences (ie smart contract invariant broken; funds stolen) 

- Subjectivity and confidence intervals (ie we believe with 69% certainty funds belong to DPRK) 

Former is desirable, latter is a big slippery slope. Of course even if a hack is publicly verifiable, if it’s not caught early then it can become very subjective quickly including who the real victims are.
tweets.full_text:
I think it’ll be a fight between:

- Objectiveness and publicly verifiable evidences (ie smart contract invariant broken; funds stolen) 

- Subjectivity and confidence intervals (ie we believe with 69% certainty funds belong to DPRK) 

Former is desirable, latter is a big slippery slope. Of course even if a hack is publicly verifiable, if it’s not caught early then it can become very subjective quickly including who the real victims are.

tweets.text:
2026 is the year enterprises require multi-model support in every AI purchasing decision.

Betting on one provider is too risky when the frontier keeps changing hands. Sometimes OpenAI is ahead, sometimes Anthropic, and for many smaller tasks an open-source model is already good enough. Enterprises do not need Einstein-level intelligence to classify an email, summarize a document, or update a spreadsheet.

At the frontier, a model can command a 90% premium. But once it is no longer at the frontier, it loses its pricing power and has to compete on cost, pushing inference toward thinner margins.

That is why the defining enterprise AI infrastructure of 2026 will be the harness: model-agnostic systems that evaluate each task and route it to the best model for intelligence, speed, security, and cost. Multi-model will become a baseline procurement requirement.

Also if intelligence really becomes a commodity that is too cheap to meter, it could generate enormous revenue while still looking economically more like a utility: massive consumption, massive infrastructure, but thin margins.

The challenge is that today’s high inference margins at the frontier help fund the enormous compute required to train the next generation of models. Compress those margins too quickly, and labs have less capital to buy compute. Less compute could mean slower progress at the frontier, creating a vicious cycle.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: Kimi K3 may be an important inflection point for AI. Potentially negative for Anthropic and OpenAI while being net positive for essentially every other company in the world. I mean that very literally. Although the real “Sputnik moment” would be an open-source frontier model that https://t.co/tdAkU4oRgo
